disease.name: Hepatocellular Carcinoma (HCC)
disease.note: Primary liver cancer arising from hepatocytes, usually in the setting of cirrhosis. In East Africa, hepatitis B is the dominant cause — vertical transmission is common. AFP screening and ultrasound surveillance in high-risk patients can detect early-stage disease. BCLC staging guides treatment. Limited access to resection and transplant in resource-limited settings.
presentingComplaints: Abdominal swelling or mass in the right upper abdomen
Pain in the right upper abdomen
Yellow discoloration of eyes or skin
pathophysiologyDetail: Hepatocarcinogenesis: chronic liver injury → hepatocyte necrosis → inflammation → regenerative hyperplasia → random genetic mutations → dysplasia → dysplastic nodules → early HCC → advanced HCC; the cirrhosis-HCC sequence takes 10-30 years

Hepatitis B integration: HBV DNA integrates into the host genome → insertional mutagenesis (HBsAg gene integrates near TERT, MLL2, CCNE1) → chromosomal instability → activation of oncogenes and silencing of tumor suppressors (p53, Rb, E-cadherin)

Aflatoxin B1: fungal metabolite from Aspergillus flavus in contaminated maize and groundnuts → metabolized by cytochrome P450 to aflatoxin-8,9-epoxide → forms DNA adducts → G:C to T:A transversion in TP53 gene at codon 249 (specific mutational hotspot) → synergistic with HBV
Telomerase reactivation: TERT promoter mutations (59% of HCC) → telomerase reactivation → limitless replicative potential → one of the earliest events in hepatocarcinogenesis

Wnt/β-catenin pathway: CTNNB1 mutations (20-40% of HCC) → constitutive β-catenin activation → nuclear translocation → transcription of MYC, CCND1, MMP7, GLUL → proliferation and metabolic reprogramming

TP53 mutations: p53 loss-of-function mutations (30-50% of HCC) → loss of G1/S checkpoint control → genomic instability → aneuploidy → aggressive tumor phenotype; G245C and R249S are aflatoxin signature mutations

Angiogenesis in HCC: VEGF, PDGF, FGF, Angiopoietin-2 overexpression → dysplastic arterialization of liver nodules → unpaired (non-portal tract-associated) arteries on histology; this is the basis for arterial phase enhancement on CT/MRI and the target of anti-angiogenic therapy

Portal vein invasion: tumor thrombus in portal vein (macrovascular invasion) → intrahepatic dissemination via portal flow → poor prognosis (BCLC C); histologic microvascular invasion is also a strong predictor of recurrence after resection

BCLC staging system: Stage 0 (very early) → Stage A (early) → Stage B (intermediate) → Stage C (advanced) → Stage D (terminal); each stage linked to specific treatment algorithms based on tumor burden, liver function, and performance status

HCC in non-cirrhotic liver: 10-20% of HCCs in Africa develop in non-cirrhotic liver — often associated with HBV infection without established cirrhosis; these patients are better candidates for resection due to preserved liver function

HCC recurrence patterns: early recurrence (<2 years) from intrahepatic metastasis of the primary tumor (aggressive biology); late recurrence (>2 years) from de novo carcinogenesis in the cirrhotic field (field cancerization effect)

Milan criteria for liver transplant: single tumor ≤5 cm OR ≤3 nodules each ≤3 cm; no macrovascular invasion; no extrahepatic disease; provides 5-year survival >70% and recurrence rate <15% — transplant is the only treatment that addresses both the tumor and the underlying cirrhosis

LIRADS (LI-RADS) imaging classification: LR-1 (benign), LR-2 (probably benign), LR-3 (indeterminate), LR-4 (probably HCC), LR-5 (definitely HCC), LR-TIV (tumor in vein); LR-5 can be diagnosed without biopsy based on typical imaging (arterial hyperenhancement + washout + capsule appearance)

HBV vaccination in East Africa: universal infant vaccination introduced in Kenya in 2001; birth dose vaccination within 24 hours prevents vertical transmission; expected to dramatically reduce HCC incidence over the next 2-3 decades; currently, 80%+ of adult HCC is HBV-related in Kenya

Aflatoxin exposure in Kenya: acute aflatoxicosis outbreaks (2004, 2010 — 317 cases, 125 deaths in Makueni and Kitui counties); chronic low-level exposure through maize and groundnuts is the norm; testing and surveillance programs exist but coverage is limited

landmarkTrials.trial: SHARP Trial (2008, New England Journal of Medicine)
landmarkTrials.population: 602 patients with advanced HCC (BCLC C) randomized to sorafenib vs placebo
landmarkTrials.intervention: Sorafenib 400 mg PO BID vs placebo
landmarkTrials.outcome: Median OS 10.7 vs 7.9 months (HR 0.69, p<0.001); median time to progression 5.5 vs 2.8 months. First systemic therapy shown to improve survival in advanced HCC.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Sorafenib available in Kenya but costly; requires preserved liver function (Child A); limited by side effects and cost

landmarkTrials.trial: IMbrave150 (2020, New England Journal of Medicine)
landmarkTrials.population: 501 patients with unresectable HCC randomized to atezolizumab + bevacizumab vs sorafenib
landmarkTrials.intervention: Atezolizumab (anti-PD-L1) 1200 mg IV + bevacizumab 15 mg/kg IV q3W vs sorafenib 400 mg PO BID
landmarkTrials.outcome: 12-month OS 67% vs 55% (HR 0.58, p<0.001); PFS 6.8 vs 4.3 months (HR 0.59). New first-line standard for advanced HCC. ORR 27% vs 12%.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: New standard; bevacizumab available in Kenya but atezolizumab is very expensive; access programs may be available

landmarkTrials.population: 1,114 patients at high risk of HCC recurrence after resection or ablation randomized to sorafenib vs placebo
landmarkTrials.intervention: Adjuvant sorafenib 400 mg PO BID for up to 4 years vs placebo
landmarkTrials.outcome: No difference in recurrence-free survival (33.4 vs 33.8 months, HR 0.94, p=0.26). Adjuvant sorafenib does NOT prevent recurrence after curative treatment.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Adjuvant sorafenib not recommended — avoids unnecessary cost and toxicity for Kenyan patients

landmarkTrials.population: 954 patients with advanced HCC randomized to lenvatinib vs sorafenib
landmarkTrials.intervention: Lenvatinib 8-12 mg PO daily (weight-based) vs sorafenib 400 mg PO BID
landmarkTrials.outcome: Lenvatinib non-inferior to sorafenib for OS (13.6 vs 12.3 months, HR 0.92). Superior for PFS (7.4 vs 3.7 months), TTP, and ORR (24% vs 9%). Alternative first-line therapy.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Lenvatinib alternative first-line; higher response rate but may not be more available or affordable in Kenya

landmarkTrials.trial: TACE vs Best Supportive Care for Intermediate HCC (1995, The Lancet — Meta-analysis of 2 RCTs)
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 2 RCTs (Llovet 2002, Lo 2002) comparing TACE vs BSC for intermediate HCC
landmarkTrials.intervention: TACE (doxorubicin/epirubicin + lipiodol + gelfoam) vs best supportive care
landmarkTrials.outcome: 2-year survival: 63% vs 27% (p=0.009) in Llovet; 31% vs 11% (p=0.002) in Lo. TACE is standard of care for BCLC B HCC.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: TACE available in Kenya at select centers; effective for intermediate HCC

landmarkTrials.trial: HCC Resection in Cirrhosis (1996, Hepatology — Multicenter)
landmarkTrials.population: 1,368 patients with HCC and cirrhosis undergoing liver resection
landmarkTrials.intervention: Liver resection for HCC in cirrhotic patients
landmarkTrials.outcome: 5-year OS 50%, operative mortality 3-5% (Child A), 15% (Child B). Predictors of poor outcome: portal hypertension, bilirubin >1 mg/dL, MELD >10, multiple tumors, satellites. Hepatectomy safe only in Child A without portal hypertension.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Resection is first-line for HCC in non-cirrhotic HBV patients in Africa; limited by expertise and ICU capacity

landmarkTrials.trial: Milan Criteria for Liver Transplantation in HCC (1996, New England Journal of Medicine)
landmarkTrials.population: 48 patients with early HCC and cirrhosis selected by Milan criteria for transplant
landmarkTrials.intervention: Orthotopic liver transplantation for HCC meeting Milan criteria
landmarkTrials.outcome: 4-year overall survival 85%, recurrence-free survival 92%. Milan criteria became the global standard for patient selection. Extended criteria (UCSF, up-to-7) have been proposed but Milan remains most widely used.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Limited relevance due to lack of liver transplant programs in Kenya; patients are referred abroad if eligible and funded

landmarkTrials.trial: RFA vs Resection for Small HCC (2012, Annals of Surgery — Multicenter RCT)
landmarkTrials.population: 200 patients with single HCC <5 cm randomized to RFA vs resection
landmarkTrials.intervention: Radiofrequency ablation vs surgical resection for small HCC
landmarkTrials.outcome: RFA: lower complication rate (5% vs 25%), shorter hospital stay (5 vs 15 days). Resection: lower local recurrence (5% vs 20% at 3 years, p<0.01). Similar 5-year OS (55% vs 50%). RFA preferred for central tumors, resection for peripheral.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: RFA available in Kenya at selected centers; good option for patients unfit for resection

evidenceTable.recommendation: First-line systemic therapy for advanced HCC
Standard of care for BCLC B

evidenceTable.therapy: RFA for small HCC (RCT vs resection)
evidenceTable.effect: 55% RFA vs 50% resection (NS); local recurrence 20% vs 5%
evidenceTable.recommendation: RFA for small/central tumors; resection for peripheral if fit

evidenceTable.therapy: Liver resection for HCC in cirrhosis
evidenceTable.recommendation: Resection for early HCC in well-compensated cirrhosis
Transplant for HCC within Milan criteria with decompensated cirrhosis

evidenceTable.therapy: HBV antiviral therapy to reduce HCC risk
evidenceTable.population: Chronic HBV on long-term antiviral therapy
evidenceTable.effect: Entecavir/tenofovir reduces HCC risk by 50-60% vs untreated
evidenceTable.recommendation: Antiviral therapy for all HBV patients with active replication

evidenceTable.therapy: AFP + US surveillance for HCC in high-risk patients
evidenceTable.effect: Surveillance increases early detection (BCLC 0/A) from 20% to 60% and curative treatment rates
evidenceTable.recommendation: 6-monthly AFP + US for all cirrhotic patients and HBV carriers

diagnosticAlgorithm: Step 1: Identify at-risk population — chronic HBV (most common in Kenya), chronic HCV, cirrhosis (any cause), alcoholic liver disease, NASH, hemochromatosis, aflatoxin exposure, family history of HCC

Step 2: Surveillance — 6-monthly abdominal ultrasound + AFP in all at-risk patients. US sensitivity 60-80% for early HCC. AFP >20 ng/mL triggers further investigation; AFP >400 ng/mL is highly suspicious. PIVKA-II can supplement AFP

Step 3: Diagnostic imaging for liver mass found on US — quadruple-phase CT or contrast-enhanced MRI (non-contrast, arterial, portal venous, delayed phases). Diagnostic hallmark: arterial hyperenhancement + portal venous/delayed washout

Step 4: LI-RADS classification — LR-3 (indeterminate): needs alternative imaging or short-term follow-up; LR-4 (probably HCC): likely HCC, consider biopsy if imaging not definitive; LR-5 (definitely HCC): can diagnose without biopsy

Step 5: AFP interpretation — >400 ng/mL + typical imaging = HCC diagnosis confirmed; 20-400 ng/mL: indeterminate, needs imaging; <20 ng/mL: does not exclude HCC (AFP-negative HCC common, 30-40% of cases)

Step 6: Liver biopsy (US/CT-guided) — only when imaging is atypical (LR-3 or LR-4) or when histology changes management; risk of bleeding (1-2%), needle tract seeding (0.5-2%); use coaxial technique to minimize seeding

Step 7: Staging — CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to detect extrahepatic spread (lung, peritoneum, lymph nodes, bone), assess portal vein patency (tumor thrombus), identify portal hypertension (varices, splenomegaly, ascites)
Step 8: BCLC staging — combines tumor burden (single/multi/invasive), Child-Pugh class (A/B/C), and ECOG PS (0-4); determines treatment allocation from curative to palliative

Step 9: Liver function assessment — Child-Pugh score (bilirubin, albumin, INR, ascites, encephalopathy) and MELD score (bilirubin, INR, creatinine) for transplant candidacy; portal hypertension assessed by hepatic venous pressure gradient (HVPG), platelet count, or presence of varices

Step 10: Multidisciplinary tumor board — review imaging, stage, liver function, and PS; determine BCLC-appropriate treatment: BCLC 0/A (resection/ablation/transplant), B (TACE), C (systemic therapy), D (best supportive care)

Kenya adaptation: AFP + US surveillance widely feasible; CT available at most provincial hospitals; TACE at select centers; no transplant program; most HCC presents at BCLC C-D; systemic therapy limited by cost; prevention (HBV vaccine + aflatoxin control) is the most impactful strategy
managementPearls: In HBV-related HCC, start antiviral therapy (entecavir or tenofovir) regardless of HCC treatment — viral suppression reduces recurrence, slows progression of cirrhosis, and reduces risk of decompensation during cancer therapy

BCLC staging is not just a staging system — it is a treatment algorithm; each stage maps to a specific first-line therapy: BCLC 0 = ablation/resection, A = resection/transplant/ablation, B = TACE, C = systemic therapy, D = supportive care

Liver resection for HCC requires both an adequate future liver remnant (FLR >30% for normal liver, >40% for cirrhotic) AND the absence of clinically significant portal hypertension (HVPG <10 mmHg, platelets >100, no varices)

For HCC meeting Milan criteria with decompensated cirrhosis (Child B/C), liver transplantation is the treatment of choice — it treats both the cancer and the underlying liver disease; in Kenya this is rarely available so ablation or TACE may be used as bridge or alternative

TACE is contraindicated in patients with main portal vein thrombus (BCLC C), Child C cirrhosis, or ECOG >2 — these patients have higher risk of liver failure after TACE and should receive systemic therapy instead

The response to TACE should be assessed with mRECIST criteria after 4-6 weeks — only target enhancing (viable) tumors; complete response = no enhancement within the target lesion; stable enhancement indicates incomplete treatment and need for repeat TACE or alternative therapy

Sorafenib side effects (hand-foot skin reaction, diarrhea, fatigue, hypertension) are common (70%+) and require active management — patient education, proactive skin care (emollients, avoid friction), anti-diarrheals, and antihypertensives are essential for adherence

Checkpoint inhibitor hepatitis: atezolizumab/bevacizumab can cause immune-mediated hepatitis (5-10%) — monitor LFTs before each cycle; hold for Grade 2 (ALT 3-5× ULN), discontinue for Grade 3+ (ALT >5× ULN); treat with steroids (prednisolone 1-2 mg/kg/day) and mycophenolate if refractory

In East Africa, where most patients present at BCLC C-D, the focus should be on: (1) HBV antiviral therapy, (2) pain and symptom control (WHO ladder, ascites management, nutritional support), (3) best supportive care with hospice referral, and (4) HBV vaccination of household contacts

Aflatoxin exposure reduction: store maize and groundnuts in cool, dry conditions; discard visibly moldy grains; sun-dry before storage; use fungicides appropriately; government aflatoxin testing programs at grain markets

HBV mother-to-child transmission prevention: all pregnant women should be screened for HBsAg; HBV-positive mothers should receive tenofovir in third trimester (28 weeks to delivery); infants should receive HBV vaccine + HBIG within 12 hours of birth — this is the single most effective HCC prevention strategy
operativeNuances: Preoperative portal vein embolization (PVE): indicated when FLR is inadequate (<30% normal, <40% cirrhotic); embolize portal vein branches to the tumor-bearing lobe → FLR hypertrophy of 10-20% over 4-6 weeks → enables safer extended hepatectomy

Intraoperative ultrasound (IOUS) of the liver is mandatory during HCC resection — it identifies additional nodules not seen on preoperative imaging (15-20% of cases), defines the transection line relative to vascular structures, and guides the precision of the resection

Pringle maneuver (portal triad clamping): intermittent clamping (15 minutes on, 5 minutes off) reduces blood loss during parenchymal transection; safe in cirrhotic patients up to 120 minutes cumulative; continuous clamping should be avoided in cirrhosis due to ischemic injury risk

Anterior approach for right hepatectomy: early division of the right hepatic artery, portal vein, and bile duct → ischemic demarcation → parenchymal transection BEFORE mobilization of the right lobe from the IVC and diaphragm; reduces tumor manipulation and blood loss, especially for large (>10 cm) HCC

In cirrhotic patients, preserve as much liver parenchyma as possible — non-anatomical (wedge) resections with 1-2 cm margins are preferred over formal segmentectomy/lobectomy when cirrhosis is present; the goal is R0 resection with adequate FLR, not anatomical precision

Laparoscopic liver resection for HCC: feasible for peripheral, left lobe, and anterior segment lesions; requires advanced laparoscopic skills; advantages: less blood loss, less pain, shorter hospital stay, fewer adhesions for future transplant. Contraindicated: large central tumors, involvement of major vasculature, severe portal hypertension

Salvage transplant: patients who develop recurrence after liver resection may still be candidates for salvage liver transplant if they meet Milan criteria; 5-year survival after salvage transplant is comparable to primary transplant (65-70%)

In East Africa, where liver resection is the most accessible curative option, a systematic approach to safe hepatectomy is critical: control inflow (Pringle), control outflow (hepatic vein), parenchymal transection with crush-clamp or CUSA, meticulous hemostasis of the cut surface, and drain placement

RFA technique for HCC: place the RFA needle into the center of the tumor under US/CT guidance; expand tines to cover the entire tumor with a 5-10 mm ablative margin; aim for impedance-controlled or temperature-controlled ablation to achieve 60-100°C at the tumor margin; monitor for bleeding and collateral damage

prognosis.bclc0aEarly: 5-year OS 60-80% with curative treatment (resection, RFA, transplant); recurrence rate 50-70% at 5 years — most recurrences treated with repeat locoregional therapy
prognosis.bclcBIntermediate: Median OS 2-3 years with TACE; 30-40% 3-year survival; retreatment needed every 6-12 months on average
prognosis.bclcCAdvanced: Median OS 10-14 months with systemic therapy (atezolizumab/bevacizumab best, sorafenib/lenvatinib alternatives); 12-month OS 55-67%
prognosis.bclcDTerminal: Median OS 3-6 months with best supportive care; focus on symptom management and quality of life; hospice referral appropriate
prognosticFactors: BCLC stage — the dominant prognostic framework; each stage has well-defined survival outcomes
Child-Pugh class — liver function is as important as tumor stage; Child A has best prognosis
Portal hypertension — clinically significant portal hypertension (HVPG ≥10) predicts decompensation after resection
Vascular invasion — both macrovascular (portal vein) and microvascular invasion strongly predict recurrence and poor survival
AFP level — AFP >400 ng/mL associated with more aggressive tumor biology and worse outcomes
Tumor size and number — single small tumors have best prognosis; multiple or bilobar disease is worse
ECOG performance status — PS 0-1 can tolerate aggressive therapy; PS 2+ limits options
Etiology of liver disease — HBV-related HCC can be controlled with antivirals; HCV-related has better prognosis after DAA treatment
Response to therapy — objective response (mRECIST) to first therapy predicts overall survival
Aflatoxin exposure — p53 mutation signature from aflatoxin associated with more aggressive tumor phenotype
prognosis.eastAfricanSpecific: Most Kenyan HCC patients present at BCLC C-D (60-70%); median survival 3-6 months; HBV is dominant cause (>80%); aflatoxin co-exposure common; limited treatment options (no transplant, limited TACE, systemic therapy costly); HBV vaccination program (2001) will reduce future burden; aflatoxin control is the primary prevention priority

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Advanced stage (BCLC C/D) -> SEVERE; early stage (BCLC 0/A) -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: Do you have known liver disease, hepatitis B, or cirrhosis?
Have you been vaccinated against hepatitis B?
Do you have pain in your right upper abdomen?
Have you lost weight unintentionally?
Do you feel full after eating only small amounts?
Has your abdomen become swollen (ascites)?
Have you noticed yellowing of your eyes or skin?
Do you have swelling in your legs?
Do you drink alcohol — chang'aa, busaa, or bottled beer?
Have you ever had a blood transfusion?
Was your mother a carrier of hepatitis B?
Have you been tested for hepatitis C?
Do you have a family history of liver cancer?
Have you noticed bruising or bleeding easily?
Do you feel confused, drowsy, or have personality changes?
Have you ever had an ultrasound of your liver?
Do you eat aflatoxin-contaminated foods (moldy maize, groundnuts)?
Are you diabetic or overweight?
Have you had any previous liver surgery or TACE?
Have you ever been screened for hepatitis B or C?
Do you have anyone in your family with liver cancer or cirrhosis?
Did your mother have hepatitis B? (vertical transmission)
Do you have itching all over your body (pruritus)?
Have you noticed your abdomen becoming larger (ascites)?
Do you have swelling in your legs or feet?
Have you ever had a blood transfusion? (HCV risk)
Do you have a history of IV drug use? (HCV risk)
Do you eat maize or groundnuts that may have been stored for a long time? (aflatoxin)
Are your maize and grains stored in a dry, ventilated place?
Have you had symptoms of confusion, drowsiness, or personality changes? (encephalopathy)
Do you bruise easily or bleed for a long time after cuts?
Have you had blood in your vomit or black stools? (variceal bleeding)
Do you have regular follow-up with a liver specialist?
Are you currently on any medications for hepatitis B or C?
Do you smoke tobacco or use any other substances? (increases liver fibrosis progression)
Do you have a history of fatty liver or been told your liver enzymes are elevated?
Have you had any imaging of your liver (ultrasound, CT, MRI)?
Do you have a history of liver abscess or other liver infections?

examination_findings.finding: Hepatomegaly — enlarged, firm, nodular liver
Hepatic bruit — auscultatory sign over liver (pathognomonic for HCC)
Ascites — shifting dullness, fluid thrill
Jaundice — scleral icterus and yellow skin
Stigmata of chronic liver disease: spider angiomas, palmar erythema
Splenomegaly from portal hypertension
Caput medusae — dilated abdominal wall veins
Gynecomastia and testicular atrophy in men
Asterixis — liver flap (encephalopathy)
Muscle wasting and cachexia
Palpable abdominal mass — irregular, nodular
Right pleural effusion (hepatic hydrothorax)
Pedal edema from hypoalbuminemia or venous compression
Fever — tumor fever or spontaneous bacterial peritonitis
Hepatomegaly — enlarged, firm, irregular/nodular liver palpable below costal margin
Hepatic bruit — systolic bruit heard over the liver (pathognomonic for HCC due to hypervascularity)
Ascites — shifting dullness, fluid thrill, positive puddle sign; can be massive
Scleral icterus / jaundice — yellow discoloration of conjunctiva and skin
Spider angiomas — multiple, over face, neck, upper chest (estrogen excess from liver failure)
Palmar erythema — reddening of thenar and hypothenar eminences
Gynecomastia — breast tissue enlargement in men from hyperestrogenism
Testicular atrophy — small, soft testes from hypogonadism in cirrhosis
Splenomegaly — congestive splenomegaly from portal hypertension
Caput medusae — dilated paraumbilical veins radiating from umbilicus (portal hypertension)
Asterixis (liver flap) — dorsiflexion of wrist with arms extended; sign of hepatic encephalopathy
Muscle wasting and cachexia — temporal, thenar, interosseous wasting from cancer cachexia
Pedal edema — pitting edema of lower extremities from hypoalbuminemia or IVC compression
Right pleural effusion (hepatic hydrothorax) — decreased breath sounds, dullness at right base
Purpura or ecchymoses — from coagulopathy and thrombocytopenia
Palpable abdominal mass — irregular, firm, nodular — may be the primary tumor itself
Dupytren contracture — palmar fascial thickening, associated with alcoholic liver disease
Clubbing and Terry's nails (leukonychia) — chronic liver disease stigmata
Fetor hepaticus — sweet, musty breath odor from dimethyl sulfide in liver failure

bedside_scores.name: Child-Pugh Score
bedside_scores.interpretation: Child A (5-6): best for resection. Child B (7-9): limited options. Child C (10-15): transplant or palliative.

bedside_scores.name: BCLC Staging System
bedside_scores.interpretation: BCLC 0/A: curative (resection/ablation/transplant). B: TACE. C: systemic therapy. D: best supportive care.

riskFactors.note: Most important risk in Africa — vertical transmission leads to chronic carrier state
Common cause of cirrhosis and HCC worldwide
80-90% of HCC develops in cirrhotic liver
Chang'aa and busaa are common hepatotoxic alcohols in Kenya
Rising cause with obesity and metabolic syndrome
Moldy maize, groundnuts — synergistic with HBV
Independent risk factor, part of metabolic syndrome
Familial clustering with HBV vertical transmission

subtypes.name: Early HCC (BCLC 0-A)
subtypes.management: Liver resection (if non-cirrhotic or Child A with good FLR). Liver transplantation (Milan criteria: single ≤5cm or up to 3 ≤3cm). Radiofrequency ablation (RFA) or Microwave ablation (MWA) for small tumors (<3cm). Percutaneous ethanol injection (PEI) alternative. TACE as bridge to transplant.

subtypes.name: Intermediate HCC (BCLC B)
subtypes.management: Transarterial Chemoembolization (TACE). Transarterial Radioembolization (TARE/Y-90). Systemic therapy if TACE contraindicated or refractory.

subtypes.name: Advanced HCC (BCLC C-D)
subtypes.management: Systemic therapy: Atezolizumab + Bevacizumab (first line). Sorafenib or Lenvatinib as alternatives. Second line: Regorafenib, Cabozantinib, Ramucirumab (if AFP >400). Palliative radiotherapy for bone metastases. Best supportive care for end-stage. Hospice referral.

mimics.whyMimic: Incidental liver mass found on imaging
Liver mass with pain, fever, weight loss
Multiple liver masses, can mimic multinodular HCC
Asymptomatic liver mass in young patients
Liver mass in young women, can be large, RUQ pain from mass effect or rupture

mimics.diseaseName: Large Regenerative or Dysplastic Nodule in Cirrhosis
mimics.whyMimic: Nodule in cirrhotic liver can be difficult to distinguish from early HCC
Liver mass with pain, weight loss, jaundice, raised CA 19-9
Incidental liver mass on imaging, can be large
Multiple liver masses in patient without known primary, can mimic multinodular HCC
Hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, portal hypertension, abnormal LFTs in endemic areas

bedside.name: ECOG performance status assessment
bedside.indication: Determine treatment candidacy and BCLC stage

laboratory.name: AFP (Alpha-fetoprotein)
laboratory.indication: Elevated >400 ng/mL strongly suggestive; >200 with mass = HCC

laboratory.name: LFTs (Bilirubin, ALT, AST, ALP, GGT, Albumin)
laboratory.indication: Assess liver function for Child-Pugh score

laboratory.name: Child-Pugh score (Bilirubin, Albumin, INR, Ascites, Encephalopathy)
laboratory.indication: Critical for prognosis and treatment decisions

laboratory.name: INR / Coagulation profile
laboratory.indication: Synthetic function; coagulopathy in liver failure

laboratory.name: Hepatitis B serology (HBsAg, HBeAg, HBV DNA)
laboratory.indication: Identify underlying cause, guide antiviral therapy

laboratory.name: PIVKA-II (Des-gamma-carboxy prothrombin)
laboratory.indication: Alternative tumor marker when AFP negative

imaging.name: Quadruple-phase CT Liver
imaging.indication: Arterial enhancement + portal venous washout = diagnostic of HCC

imaging.name: Contrast-enhanced MRI Liver with hepatobiliary agent
imaging.indication: Best characterization if CT equivocal; problem-solving

imaging.name: Abdominal Ultrasound with Doppler
imaging.indication: Screening tool; assess portal vein patency

imaging.name: PET-CT
imaging.indication: Detect occult metastases; not as useful for HCC

resuscitation: If acute presentation (tumor rupture): ABC, large bore IV access, IV fluids, cross-match 4-6 units, urgent CT, consider embolization or emergency laparotomy
If variceal bleed: standard variceal bleed protocol (Terlipressin, antibiotics, endoscopy)
If encephalopathy: lactulose, rifaximin, stop sedatives
conservative: Pain control: WHO ladder — paracetamol, then tramadol, then morphine. Avoid NSAIDs (risk of variceal bleeding, hepatorenal)
Management of ascites: spironolactone ± furosemide, low-sodium diet, therapeutic paracentesis
Antiviral therapy: entecavir or tenofovir for HBV; DAA for HCV
Nutritional support: high protein diet (unless encephalopathy), vitamin K if coagulopathic

surgical.resection: Child A cirrhosis or non-cirrhotic, single tumor, adequate future liver remnant (FLR >30%)
surgical.transplant: Milan criteria (single ≤5cm or ≤3 nodules all ≤3cm), Child B/C, no contraindications
surgical.ablation: RFA/MWA for small tumors (<3cm), tumors not resectable, as bridge to transplant

nonOperative: TACE: for BCLC B (intermediate), multinodular disease
TARE (Y-90): alternative to TACE, for portal vein thrombosis patients
Systemic therapy: Atezolizumab + Bevacizumab (first line), Sorafenib/Lenvatinib (alternative first line)
Second line: Regorafenib, Cabozantinib, Ramucirumab (if AFP >400)

general: ICU monitoring — 24-48 hours after liver resection
Monitor liver function, INR, Hb, platelet count
Monitor drain output for bile leak
IV fluids, blood products as needed
Nutrition: early enteral feeding if possible
VTE prophylaxis — SC enoxaparin (caution in cirrhotics)
discharge_criteria: No bile leak or drain output <50 mL/day

pathways.criteria: Single tumor or up to 3 <3cm, Child A/B, PS 0
pathways.treatment: Resection (non-cirrhotic or Child A). Transplant (Milan criteria). RFA/MWA. TACE as bridge to transplant.
TACE. TARE (Y-90 radioembolization). Consider systemic therapy if TACE refractory.

pathways.criteria: Vascular invasion, metastases, Child B/C, PS 1-2
pathways.treatment: Systemic therapy: Atezolizumab + Bevacizumab. Sorafenib or Lenvatinib. Best supportive care for end-stage.

operative_templates.indication: HCC in right lobe, Child A cirrhosis or non-cirrhotic, adequate FLR
steps: Patient supine, with arm extended, +/- bilateral subcostal incision
Bilateral subcostal incision with midline extension (Mercedes incision) or J-incision
Abdominal exploration — exclude extrahepatic disease, assess cirrhosis severity
Intraoperative ultrasound — locate tumor, mark transection line, assess vascular involvement
Mobilize liver: divide falciform, triangular, and coronary ligaments
Identify and ligate right hepatic artery and right portal vein (extrahepatic approach)
Dissect right hepatic vein between liver and IVC
Pringle maneuver (clamping portal triad) for inflow control if needed
Parenchymal transection using CUSA/harmonic scalpel — follow ischemic demarcation line
Clip/ligate crossing vessels and bile ducts
Apply fibrin sealant to cut surface
Check hemostasis and bile leak (Valsalva + white test)
operative_templates.approach: Open laparotomy (bilateral subcostal) or Laparoscopic (selected cases)

dischargeCriteria: Liver function stable or improving (bilirubin, INR trending down)
Drain output <50 mL/day (non-billous) — drain can be removed
Ascites controlled with diuretics if present
Mobilizing independently or with baseline assistance
Follow-up imaging and surveillance plan confirmed

patient_education.explanation: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is cancer of the liver cells. It usually develops in people with chronic liver disease (cirrhosis) from hepatitis B, hepatitis C, alcohol, or fatty liver. In East Africa, hepatitis B infection passed from mother to child is the most common cause. Early detection through ultrasound and blood tests (AFP) every 6 months is essential for people at risk.
patient_education.warningSigns: Go to the hospital immediately if: you have sudden severe right upper abdominal pain, you vomit blood or pass black stools, you become confused or extremely drowsy, you develop rapid abdominal swelling, or you have high fever with chills.
patient_education.activity: Rest as needed during treatment. Avoid heavy lifting. Do not drink alcohol at all — it worsens liver damage. Get vaccinated against hepatitis B if not immune. Avoid traditional herbal medicines — many are toxic to the liver.
patient_education.diet: Eat a balanced diet with adequate protein. If you have ascites: limit salt intake. If you have encephalopathy: limit protein as advised by your doctor. Avoid raw or undercooked seafood. Do not use herbal supplements without checking with your doctor. Milk thistle and other liver supplements have not been proven effective.
patient_education.medication: Take antiviral medication (entecavir/tenofovir) lifelong if hepatitis B positive. Take diuretics as prescribed for ascites. Take lactulose if prescribed for encephalopathy. Do not take NSAIDs (ibuprofen, diclofenac). Avoid or limit paracetamol to no more than 2g per day.
patient_education.followUp: Surveillance: AFP and ultrasound every 6 months if at risk. After treatment: CT/MRI every 3-6 months for the first 2 years, then every 6-12 months. Monitor for recurrence. Hepatology follow-up for cirrhosis management.

before_discharge: Treatment plan discussed with patient and family (BCLC stage, treatment options)
Antiviral therapy started if HBV positive
Hepatitis B vaccination for household contacts

after_discharge.action: Clinical review — symptom assessment, LFTs, AFP
Post-treatment imaging: CT or MRI liver
Surveillance: AFP + US or CT; assess treatment response
Surveillance imaging and AFP; Child-Pugh/MELD re-assessment
Annual surveillance; monitor for recurrence and progression of cirrhosis

when_to_return: Vomiting blood or passing black stools
Yellowing of eyes or skin worsening
